metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `...` in AC4's structured degradation entry with the full required field set, e.g. `{kind: "outbound-reporter-not-permitted", jira_key: str, reporter_account_id: str | None, reason: str}`, so the assertion target is fully specified.
findings.finding: AC4's structured degradation entry schema is partially specified as `{kind: "outbound-reporter-not-permitted", ...}` — the '...' is an unresolved placeholder. An executor doesn't know what other fields the entry must contain (e.g., `jira_key`, `assignee`, `reason`, `field`), making the AC's assertion untestable without guessing the schema.
findings.suggested_fix: State the full schema inline, modeled on the existing assignee-unresolved soft-fail entry (reference that schema explicitly so the executor can mirror it): e.g. `{kind: "outbound-reporter-not-permitted", jira_key: <str>, reporter_account_id: <str|None>, reason: "permission-denied"}`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `assignee_pre_resolved: bool = False` to the `OutboundMutation` dataclass (or document an alternative carrier) so the fast-path flag can be set and read without a TypeError on the frozen dataclass.
findings.finding: The plan's `assignee_pre_resolved` flag mechanism relies on carrying an extra boolean field on an `OutboundMutation`, but `OutboundMutation` is a `frozen=True` dataclass with a fixed set of fields (`local_id, jira_key, action, fields, comments, labels, links`) and no catch-all. Setting `assignee_pre_resolved: true` on the mutation (as specified) will raise a `TypeError` at construction time unless the dataclass is extended.
findings.location: Section 2 (Outbound assignee by accountId) — 'the differ also sets an explicit boolean `assignee_pre_resolved: true` on the mutation'; outbound_differ.py:228-238 (OutboundMutation dataclass)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `assignee_pre_resolved: bool = dataclass_field(default=False)` to `OutboundMutation` and update `file_impact` to include `outbound_differ.py` under the dataclass-schema change, not only the differ logic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and add to `file_impact` the plumbing in `dispatch_one.py` (and/or `update_one` signature) that carries the `assignee_pre_resolved` flag from the mutation dict through to `AcliClient.update_issue` so the skip gate can actually fire.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `update_one` / `_update_one_scalar_update` (in `dispatch_one.py`) calls `client.update_issue`, and that `AcliClient.update_issue` (~line 354) re-resolves the assignee via `validate_assignee_exists`. This is confirmed. However, the plan's proposed skip gate ('`update_issue` SKIPS `validate_assignee_exists` exactly when `assignee_pre_resolved` flag is set') requires `dispatch_one.update_one` (or `_update_one_scalar_update`) to inspect the flag and pass it into `client.update_issue`, but `update_one` currently receives only the mutation dict and passes `**fields` directly to `client.update_issue` — there is no plumbing for an out-of-band flag from the differ to the `AcliClient.update_issue` method. The plan's `file_impact` lists `acli.py` but not the intermediary `dispatch_one.py` for this plumbing, yet `dispatch_one.py` owns the call site.
findings.location: Section 2 — 'update_issue SKIPS validate_assignee_exists … exactly when that flag is set'; dispatch_one.py:605 (`_call_with_retry(client.update_issue, issue_key, **fields)`); acli.py:325-357

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `batch_dispatch.py` to `file_impact` and specify that `_mutation_to_batch_dict` must propagate the `assignee_pre_resolved` flag from `OutboundMutation` into the batch dict so `update_one`/`client.update_issue` can consume it.
findings.finding: `batch_dispatch.py` is a missing edit target: `_mutation_to_batch_dict` (batch_dispatch.py line 71) converts an `OutboundMutation` into the legacy batch dict consumed by `update_one`; the new `assignee_pre_resolved` flag on the mutation must be threaded through this conversion so `update_one` (dispatch_one.py) can pass it to `client.update_issue` to suppress re-resolution. Without a `batch_dispatch.py` edit, the flag is silently dropped.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_mutation_to_batch_dict`, when `action == 'update'`, also extract and forward `payload.get('assignee_pre_resolved', False)` as a top-level key on the returned dict; then in `update_one` / `_update_one_scalar_update`, pass it as a kwarg to `client.update_issue`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `dispatch_one.py` (or `acli.py`) as an additional edit target for reporter-permission error isolation — the 403/400 guard must be applied at the call site in `_update_one_scalar_update` or `client.update_issue`, not only in `apply_handlers.py`.
findings.finding: The reporter 403/400 soft-fail is attributed to `apply_handlers.py`, but the Jira call that would raise the permission error lives inside `_call_with_retry(client.update_issue, ...)` in `_update_one_scalar_update` (dispatch_one.py). The current `handle_update` only catches 404 HTTPErrors and `AssigneeNotFoundError`; a reporter-field 403 would propagate as an unhandled `urllib.error.HTTPError` and abort the mutation. The plan does not specify that `dispatch_one.py` needs a reporter-specific 403 guard (only its allowlist change is named), leaving the degradation path incomplete.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `assignee_pre_resolved: bool = False` to the `OutboundMutation` dataclass declaration (it is `frozen=True` so the field must be declared there, not set post-construction) and document that `update_one` reads it from the mutation dict to skip `validate_assignee_exists` in `client.update_issue`.
findings.finding: The `OutboundMutation` dataclass is `frozen=True` with a fixed schema (`fields`, `comments`, `labels`, `links`) — it has no `assignee_pre_resolved` slot. The plan's flag-threading mechanism requires adding this field to the dataclass, but the plan does not call this out as a required schema change. Without it, the flag cannot be written at the differ and read by `update_one`/`update_issue`.
findings.location: Section 2 (Outbound assignee by accountId) / `outbound_differ.py` OutboundMutation dataclass
findings.suggested_fix: In `OutboundMutation`: add `assignee_pre_resolved: bool = dataclass_field(default=False)`. In `_diff_fields` / `_assignee_resolver`, construct `OutboundMutation(..., assignee_pre_resolved=True)` when the accountId fast-path resolved. In `update_one` → `_update_one_scalar_update`, read `mutation.get('assignee_pre_resolved', False)` and, when True, call `client.update_issue(jira_key, assignee=acct)` bypassing `validate_assignee_exists` inside `AcliClient.update_issue`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `reporter` is popped from fields BEFORE the allowlist filter in `update_one` / `_update_one_apply_parent`-equivalent phase (mirroring the `parent` pop pattern), dispatched via a dedicated `client.set_reporter(jira_key, accountId)` REST call, and that the 403/400 from that call alone is caught and converted to a structured soft-fail entry — leaving the remaining scalar fields to proceed via the existing `client.update_issue` path.
findings.finding: The reporter 403/400 interception mechanism is broken as specified. The plan routes `reporter` through the standard `update_issue(**fields)` call (via the allowlist), then says a 403/400 on the reporter field is swallowed. But `client.update_issue` issues a single REST call for all fields together — Jira returns a top-level 403/400 that aborts the entire mutation, not a field-specific reporter error. A 403 on Modify-Reporter will therefore cause the whole ticket update to fail (not just the reporter field). Reporter needs its own dedicated REST call (mirroring how `parent` is popped before the allowlist and routed through `client.set_parent`), so that its 403 can be caught and soft-failed independently without aborting the scalar field update.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the `{kind: 'outbound-reporter-not-permitted', ...}` schema to list all required fields (e.g. `kind`, `key`, `local_id`, `reporter`, `pass_id`, `timestamp_ns`, `reason`) in What §4 or AC4, matching the existing alert-store field conventions.
findings.finding: The structured degradation entry `{kind: 'outbound-reporter-not-permitted', ...}` introduced in AC4 and What §4 has its field vocabulary left as '...' — an implementer must guess the exact fields by analogy with the existing `outbound-update-assignee-unresolved` kind; the schema is referenced but never defined.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to What §4: 'The alert entry MUST carry at minimum: `{kind: "outbound-reporter-not-permitted", key: <jira_key>, local_id: <local_id>, reporter: <accountId_or_None>, pass_id: <pass_id>, timestamp_ns: <ns>, reason: <str>}`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact mechanism by which `assignee_pre_resolved` travels from the differ to `update_issue`: either (a) add the field to `OutboundMutation` and show the conversion path through `update_one` → `update_issue`, or (b) store the pre-resolved accountId in a known location (e.g. a sentinel shape in `changed['assignee']`) that `update_issue` can detect without a separate flag.
findings.finding: The `assignee_pre_resolved: true` flag is specified as being set 'on the mutation' by the differ, and `update_issue` is to 'skip `validate_assignee_exists` exactly when that flag is set' — but `OutboundMutation` is a `frozen=True` dataclass with no such field, and the mechanism by which the flag travels from the differ through `update_one`'s `mutation` dict into `update_issue`'s `**kwargs` is never specified.
findings.location: What §2 — 'the differ also sets an explicit boolean `assignee_pre_resolved: true` on the mutation' and 'update_issue SKIPS validate_assignee_exists … exactly when that flag is set'

findings.finding: The mechanism for threading a fast-path accountId from _assignee_resolver into the changed dict and past AcliClient.update_issue's re-resolution is underspecified and may not work as described. Currently _diff_fields writes `local_val` (the raw local string) to `changed['assignee']`, not the resolved accountId returned by the resolver. The plan says to 'thread the already-resolved accountId through so update_issue SKIPS re-resolution,' but does not specify: (a) that _diff_fields must write `acct` (the resolved accountId) rather than `local_val` into `changed` when a fast-path resolution succeeds, and (b) how update_issue distinguishes an already-resolved accountId to skip validate_assignee_exists. The shape-detection fallback ('detect the accountId shape') has no stable contract — Jira accountIds are opaque server strings with no guaranteed format. Without an explicit pre-resolved flag propagated from differ → mutation → update_issue, the fast-path accountId lands in validate_assignee_exists as a query string, which searches by name/email/accountId — a search-by-accountId may or may not return a result depending on the Jira instance, defeating the purpose.
findings.location: What / Section 2 (outbound assignee by accountId) — _assignee_resolver fast-path + update_issue skip-re-resolution mechanism
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `assignee_account_id` (or `assignee_pre_resolved: bool`) field to OutboundMutation (or as a parallel key in the fields dict). In _diff_fields: when the resolver returns (acct, True) and acct is non-None, write `changed['assignee'] = acct` AND set `changed['assignee_pre_resolved'] = True`. In AcliClient.update_issue: pop `assignee_pre_resolved`; if True, skip the validate_assignee_exists call and use the accountId directly with the unassign_issue / REST assignee PUT path.

findings.finding: The plan states (Section 4) that `reporter` must be added to the 'outbound field-mapping layer (`outbound_fields.py`)'. However, `_map_local_to_jira_fields` in `outbound_fields.py` maps fields that already exist on the local ticket dict (title→summary, description, status, assignee, etc.). Local tickets have no `reporter` field in their schema — the plan does not identify what local-ticket field maps to `reporter`, nor how the identity-resolved accountId is injected into the mapping layer rather than resolved at apply time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `reporter` must NOT be added to `_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST` (the ACLI-edit gate); instead, route reporter updates via a dedicated REST PUT path in `apply_handlers.py` (mirroring the `priority`/`parent` REST-bypass pattern) and intercept the reporter field before it reaches `_update_one_scalar_update`.
findings.finding: The plan's Section 4 instruction to add `reporter` to `_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST` in `dispatch_one.py` would route `reporter` through ACLI's `jira workitem edit --reporter` flag, which ACLI does not expose. The existing allowlist gates fields dispatched via the ACLI subprocess edit path; `reporter` must be set via REST PUT (like `priority` and `parent`) and should NOT enter the ACLI allowlist. Adding it there would cause ACLI to reject the edit with a non-zero exit or silently no-op, defeating the feature.
findings.location: Section 4 ('Reporter by accountId where permitted') / dispatch_one.py `_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST`
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the `priority`/`parent` pattern: pop `reporter` from `fields` before `_update_one_filter_fields` in `update_one`, and dispatch it via `client.set_reporter(jira_key, accountId)` using `_direct_rest_put_raw("/rest/api/3/issue/{key}", {"fields":{"reporter":{"accountId":accountId}}})`. Do not add `reporter` to `_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST`.

findings.finding: Section 4's reporter permission detection asserts that `403/400` on the reporter field means 'not granted' and should be swallowed. In Jira Cloud REST API v3, a restricted-field error inside a multi-field PUT (`/rest/api/3/issue/{key}`) is returned as HTTP 400 with a JSON body containing a field-level errors object (e.g. `{"errors":{"reporter":"Field 'reporter' cannot be set."}}`), NOT as a 403. A 403 on that same call means the token lacks issue-edit permission entirely — swallowing it would silently skip ALL updates, not just the reporter field. The plan conflates these two distinct error shapes.
findings.suggested_fix: Set reporter in a dedicated sub-call (`PUT /rest/api/3/issue/{key}` with only `{fields:{reporter:{accountId:...}}}`) so a 400/403 is unambiguously reporter-specific and can be swallowed without affecting other field mutations.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify exactly where `assignee_pre_resolved` lives in the data flow: either as a new field on `OutboundMutation` (requires unfreezing or using `field(default=False)`) or as a top-level mutation dict key distinct from `fields`, and confirm it is not stripped by `_update_one_filter_fields` before `acli.update_issue` sees it.
findings.finding: The plan proposes setting a boolean `assignee_pre_resolved: true` on the mutation (Section 2b) to signal `update_issue` to skip `validate_assignee_exists`. However, `OutboundMutation` is a **frozen** dataclass (`@dataclass(frozen=True)`, outbound_differ.py:228). Adding `assignee_pre_resolved` requires explicitly declaring it as a field on the frozen dataclass — it cannot be set as an ad-hoc attribute after creation. The plan's prose describes the flag as being set on 'the mutation's changed["assignee_pre_resolved"]' dict, which would work if carried in `fields`, but then `_update_one_filter_fields` would strip it (it's not in `_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST`). The threading path is under-specified and at risk of the flag being silently dropped before reaching `update_issue`.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `assignee_pre_resolved: bool = False` as an explicit field to the `OutboundMutation` dataclass, and thread it as a separate kwarg to `update_one` and then to `client.update_issue` — NOT inside the `fields` dict that goes through `_update_one_filter_fields`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a decision rule for the multi-result case in What §3: either 'degrade to the existing search path (treat multi-result as a miss)' or 'select only when exactly one result is returned', and add a corresponding AC3 test stub.
findings.finding: Section 3's best-effort `/user/search` bootstrap specifies a fallback for zero results ('hidden email degrades to unassigned') but does not specify what to do when `/rest/api/3/user/search?query=<email>` returns multiple accounts — an ambiguous result that could cause incorrect accountId selection.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to What §3: 'When /user/search returns more than one account, treat it as a miss (degrade to the existing assignee-search path) — do NOT select results[0].'

findings.finding: Section 3's best-effort `/user/search` bootstrap specifies zero-result (miss) and hidden-email fallbacks, but is silent on the multi-result case: when `/rest/api/3/user/search?query=<email>` returns more than one user object, no selection or rejection rule is defined.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to §3: 'If `/user/search` returns more than one result, treat as ambiguous and fall through to the existing search/unassigned path (same as zero results). Only a unique single result is accepted for bootstrapping.' Mirror the unique-match rule already used in `validate_assignee_exists`'s normalized-match step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact recovery mechanism for a reporter 403/400 in Section 4: specify whether the implementation should (a) attempt `update_issue` with reporter, catch 403/400 and retry without reporter (documenting the double-call cost), (b) always split reporter into a separate call and swallow its failure, or (c) check permissions via a pre-flight call — and update `file_impact` if a new file is touched.
findings.finding: Section 4 says a 403/400 on the reporter field is 'treated as not granted and swallowed', but doesn't specify the recovery mechanism: does the executor (a) attempt the full `update_issue` call with `reporter` included, catch a 403/400, then retry the same call WITHOUT `reporter`, or (b) make two separate calls (one with reporter, one without), or (c) make one call without reporter after detecting the permission gap via a pre-check? Jira typically returns a full-response 403 (not a field-level error) for reporter permission failures, so option (a) involves a second Jira API call and different error-handling logic from options (b)/(c). An executor choosing the wrong model will produce either double API calls or a missing retry.
findings.location: Section 4 (Reporter by accountId where permitted): 'Detect the permission best-effort (a 403/400 on the reporter field is treated as "not granted" and swallowed).'
findings.suggested_fix: Recommend option (b) — a dedicated reporter-set call (`PUT /rest/api/3/issue/{key}` with only `{fields:{reporter:{accountId:...}}}`), issued after the main `update_issue` call, whose 403/400 is caught, logged as the structured degradation entry, and not re-raised — so the main update is never retried and write volume is bounded.

findings.finding: Section 4 states reporter permission detection relies on 'a 403/400 on the reporter field is treated as not granted and swallowed', but Jira Cloud returns HTTP 400 (not 403) when a user attempts to set a reporter without MODIFY_REPORTER project permission. The error appears in the body's `errors.reporter` field as a 400 response. If the implementation catches only 403 it will miss the actual permission-denied signal; if it catches only 400 it may swallow legitimate 400s (e.g. invalid accountId). The plan conflates both without specifying which to catch.
findings.location: Section 4 ('Reporter by accountId where permitted') / apply_handlers.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the Jira Cloud `/rest/api/3/user/search?query=<email>` endpoint: confirm it is accessible with the service-account token scope in use, document the exact response shape for a hidden-email user, and confirm it is distinct from (or the same as) `/rest/api/3/users/search`.
findings.finding: The plan's Section 3 best-effort bootstrap asserts that `/rest/api/3/user/search?query=<email>` will resolve a Jira accountId from an email, but this endpoint is distinct from the existing `/rest/api/3/user/assignable/search` the codebase uses — and it is a different endpoint from Jira Cloud's current user-search surface (`/rest/api/3/users/search` is the v3 general-user search; `/rest/api/3/user/search` is present but its scope, required permissions, response shape, and behavior under 'hidden email' (Atlassian privacy) are unverified in the plan. The plan cites 'hidden email (privacy) ... degrades gracefully' but doesn't verify the actual API response shape when email is hidden.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state that `changed['assignee']` is an internal mutation object with `update_issue` as its sole consumer (or enumerate all consumers), acknowledge the shape change, and add a back-out note (e.g., 'revert the flag and differ change as a unit if downstream issues surface').
findings.finding: The mutation shape change — `changed['assignee']` carrying a resolved opaque accountId instead of the raw local string — is not acknowledged as a potentially breaking change for consumers of that field beyond `update_issue`, and no rollback/back-out plan is stated. The plan notes accountIds are opaque and warns against shape-detection, implying awareness of downstream risk, but does not enumerate or clear other consumers.
findings.location: What §2 ('Outbound assignee by accountId'); acli.py / outbound_differ.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to What §2: 'changed["assignee"] is consumed exclusively by update_issue within this reconcile path; no other consumer reads it directly. To roll back: revert the assignee_pre_resolved flag logic in outbound_differ.py and acli.py as a single unit, restoring the raw local string in changed["assignee"].'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the rate-limit/retry policy for the best-effort `/user/search` call in Section 3 (e.g., 'single call, no retry; a non-2xx response or timeout degrades to the existing path without logging a warning beyond DEBUG level') so the executor has a concrete default to implement.
findings.finding: The best-effort `/user/search` call in Section 3 has no stated rate-limit or retry policy. At reconcile time this call may be issued once per ticket per sync run; at scale (many tickets, many identities) it could hit Jira's rate limits. No constraint is stated and no default is provided.
findings.location: Section 3: 'a best-effort `/rest/api/3/user/search?query=<email>` lookup MAY resolve the accountId at reconcile time'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Section 3: 'Single call per identity per sync run, no retry. A non-2xx response (including 429) is treated identically to a zero-result response: degrade to the existing search/unassigned path. Log at DEBUG only.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all consumers of the mutation dict's `changed['assignee']` field that currently expect a local display string, and for each either (a) verify they tolerate an opaque accountId, or (b) specify an adaptation (e.g., use a separate `changed['assignee_display']` key to preserve the human-readable name alongside the resolved accountId).
findings.finding: The plan changes the value written into the mutation's `changed['assignee']` from the raw local string to a resolved accountId when the fast-path resolves (Section 2, step (a): 'the differ writes the RESOLVED accountId (acct) — not the raw local string — into the mutation's changed[\'assignee\']'). The plan specifies only one consumer that is adapted to this semantic change (`update_issue` via the `assignee_pre_resolved` flag). Other consumers of the mutation dict — logging, audit-trail writers, diff-display, any downstream step in the reconcile pipeline that reads `changed['assignee']` — are not enumerated or verified to tolerate receiving an opaque accountId instead of a human-readable local string.
findings.location: Section 2 — Outbound assignee by accountId (outbound_differ.py); step (a) and (c)
findings.suggested_fix: Consider keeping the raw local string in `changed['assignee']` for display/audit purposes and introducing a parallel key such as `changed['assignee_account_id']` that carries the resolved accountId. The `assignee_pre_resolved` flag then directs `update_issue` to use `assignee_account_id` instead of re-resolving `assignee`. This avoids semantic breakage for all existing consumers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC test for the reporter happy path: given a stubbed response where Modify-Reporter is permitted, assert that `update_issue` is called with the resolved accountId in the reporter field (not merely that no error is raised).
findings.finding: The reporter AC only mandates testing the permission-denied (failure) path; there is no required test for the happy path where Modify-Reporter IS granted and the reporter field is successfully set by accountId.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC 4 with: 'test (stubbed grant): when Modify-Reporter is permitted, assert the mutation payload contains `reporter: <accountId>` and no soft-fail entry for reporter is recorded.'

findings.finding: AC3 (bootstrap /user/search) specifies only two test scenarios — 'returns an accountId' (single result) and 'miss (hidden email)' — but omits the case where /user/search returns multiple results (ambiguous match), leaving the disambiguation or tie-breaking behavior unspecified and untested.
findings.suggested_fix: Treat a multi-result response as a miss (same degradation path as hidden-email): 'if len(results) != 1: return None' — this is safe, unambiguous, and consistent with the 'exact accountId' principle established in the design.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete `pytest` invocation (with test file path and test function name, e.g. `pytest tests/test_identity.py::test_resolve_mapping_hit_and_miss -v`) to each AC so the proving command is mechanically runnable and reviewable.
findings.finding: No concrete proving command (e.g., a `pytest` invocation with a test file path) is given for any of the four ACs. All tests are described in prose ('a test asserts…', 'test: fixture + stubbed…') without specifying test module paths, test function names, or a runnable command that produces evidence on success. This means a reviewer cannot mechanically verify that the stated tests exist and pass.
findings.suggested_fix:
For each AC, append a line such as:
- AC1: `pytest tests/_commands/test_identity.py::test_resolve_mapping -v`
- AC2: `pytest tests/_engine/test_outbound_differ.py::test_assignee_accountid_fastpath tests/_engine/test_outbound_differ.py::test_assignee_unresolved_degrades -v`
- AC3: `pytest tests/_engine/test_acli_rest.py::test_user_search_bootstrap_hit tests/_engine/test_acli_rest.py::test_user_search_bootstrap_miss -v`
- AC4: `pytest tests/_engine/test_apply_handlers.py::test_reporter_not_permitted_structured_degradation -v`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether 'bootstrapping the mapping' means the resolved accountId is persisted back to the identity entity (requiring a file_impact entry and an AC assertion for write-back) or only used in-flight for the current reconcile (rename to 'best-effort in-flight resolution' and drop the 'bootstrapping' language).
findings.finding: 'Bootstrapping the mapping' in What §3 implies the resolved accountId is written back to the identity entity's mappings, but no file in file_impact handles this write-back, and AC3 only tests resolution behavior — not persistence. If 'bootstrapping' means write-back, the AC is an incomplete verifier and a persistence file is missing; if it means only in-flight use, the term is misleading and creates a cross-section contradiction.
findings.suggested_fix: If in-flight only: replace 'bootstrapping the mapping' with 'resolving in-flight for this reconcile cycle' in What §3. If write-back is intended: add the identity-persistence module to file_impact and extend AC3 to assert that a newly resolved accountId is written to the identity's mappings.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Section 3 whether the /user/search-resolved accountId is (a) transient (used only for the current reconcile run, re-resolved each time) or (b) persisted back to the identity store; if (b), specify the write-back file/path, ownership, idempotency guard, and update the file_impact list accordingly; if (a), remove the word 'bootstrapping' to avoid misleading the implementer.
findings.finding: The 'bootstrapping the mapping' language in Section 3 implies the /user/search-resolved accountId is persisted back into the identity store (so future reconciles skip /user/search), but the plan specifies no write-back path, no ownership of that write, and no concurrency guard against concurrent reconcile runs both resolving and writing the same mapping. If the resolved accountId is only used transiently within one reconcile run, the 'bootstrapping' framing is misleading and the AC ('lets the reconciler resolve the assignee by that accountId') only proves transient resolution — not a durable bootstrap. The lifecycle of this derived state (CREATE by whom, UPDATE guard, RETIRE path) is entirely unspecified.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'bootstrapping the mapping' with 'resolving the accountId transiently for this reconcile run' if write-back is not intended. If write-back IS intended, add a file_impact entry for the identity store writer, specify that the write is a CAS/idempotent upsert (add mapping only if absent), and add an AC asserting durability across two reconcile calls.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC or documented validation step that provides an observable, non-stub signal — e.g., a named integration-test fixture that exercises the accountId path against a canned Jira sandbox, or an explicit dogfooding step — so the value claim is verifiable beyond offline stubs.
findings.finding: No concrete validation mechanism is specified for the improvement's value: all ACs rely solely on offline stubs; there is no before/after workflow comparison, operational metric, or dogfooding criterion that would confirm correct assignee/reporter resolution in a live or integration context.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `jira_account_id`'s email-match leg delegates to the existing `_match_by_email` helper (or a shared private scan primitive) rather than reimplementing `os.listdir` + `_reduce` + case-insensitive email comparison independently.
findings.finding: `jira_account_id` as plan-described must scan all identity ticket directories and reduce each, duplicating the `_match_by_email` scan-and-reduce loop that already exists in `identity.py` (lines 119–153). The plan does not direct reuse of `_match_by_email` for the email-match leg of `jira_account_id`, resulting in two parallel implementations of the same `os.listdir` + `_reduce` + case-insensitive email comparison pattern.
findings.location: Section 1 (`src/rebar/_commands/identity.py`) — `jira_account_id` spec; vs. existing `_match_by_email` at identity.py:119–153
findings.suggested_fix: In `jira_account_id`, call `_match_by_email(local_assignee, tracker)` for the email-match leg (it already returns the sole matching identity id or None with ambiguity handling). Only add the ticket_id-direct-match leg (`_is_identity(local_assignee, tracker)`) as the new code path, then extract the `{provider:"jira"}` mapping from the resolved identity's reduced state.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly that the `jira_account_id(assignee)` identity-mapping lookup runs BEFORE the `_assignee_cache` check (or that both paths write into the same cache), so repeat occurrences of the same assignee also benefit from the fast-path and the cache never serves a stale pre-identity-mapping result.
findings.finding: The `_assignee_resolver`'s `_assignee_cache` is checked BEFORE the identity-mapping fast-path. The plan says 'try the identity mapping FIRST: if `jira_account_id(assignee)` returns an accountId, return `(accountId, authoritative=True)` — no Jira search needed. Only when there is NO identity mapping fall back to the existing `client.validate_assignee_exists` search path.' But the existing resolver (outbound_differ.py:473-501) checks `_assignee_cache` at line 485 BEFORE any lookup. On a second call for the same assignee string the cached result (from the first-pass `validate_assignee_exists` lookup) is returned, bypassing the identity-mapping path entirely on all subsequent calls.
findings.suggested_fix: Place the `jira_account_id(assignee)` call before the `if assignee in _assignee_cache` guard, and write its result into `_assignee_cache[assignee]` so subsequent calls for the same assignee also get the identity-mapping accountId without re-calling `jira_account_id`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the 'Why' (or a new 'Approach rationale') section explaining why the identity-entity `{provider, external_id}` mapping is the right home (e.g., provider-neutral reuse by 2f13, already-established entity boundary, avoids coupling accountId to a single Jira binding).
findings.finding: The plan provides a 'Why' rationale for why NOT email/display-name matching, but no positive rationale for why the local identity-entity `{provider, external_id}` mapping structure (vs. alternatives such as a dedicated bridge_state accountId cache or enriching bindings.json) is the right architectural home.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate `GET /rest/api/3/mypermissions?projectKey=<key>&permissions=MODIFY_REPORTER` (Jira Cloud prior art) as the permission probe before committing to trial-error 403/400 detection for reporter permission; document the decision either way.
findings.finding: The plan proposes detecting 'Modify Reporter' permission by observing a 403/400 on the reporter field write (trial-and-error detection), but Jira Cloud REST API v3 already provides GET /rest/api/3/mypermissions?issueKey=<key> which returns exact project-permission flags including MODIFY_REPORTER. The plan does not reference or consciously reject this prior-art approach, so it may be reinventing a noisier behavioral heuristic when a deterministic API exists.
findings.location: What > Section 4 (Reporter by accountId where permitted)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a per-project `_has_modify_reporter_permission(project_key)` helper that calls `_direct_rest_get('/rest/api/3/mypermissions?projectKey=<key>&permissions=MODIFY_REPORTER')` once per pass and caches the result; use the boolean to gate the reporter write rather than catching 403 per issue.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether passing a pre-resolved accountId directly through `changed['assignee']` (relying on `validate_assignee_exists`'s existing exact-accountId short-circuit at acli.py lines 568–579) is sufficient to skip fuzzy search, and justify the `assignee_pre_resolved` flag only if that path is genuinely insufficient.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel two-channel pattern — writing the resolved accountId into `changed['assignee']` AND setting a separate `assignee_pre_resolved: bool` flag on the mutation — to skip `validate_assignee_exists` in `update_issue`. However, `OutboundMutation` is a `frozen=True` dataclass with no `assignee_pre_resolved` field, so the flag has no natural home in the current type. More importantly, `validate_assignee_exists` already performs an exact `accountId` match as its first step (acli.py lines 568–579), meaning a pre-resolved accountId passed as `kwargs['assignee']` to `update_issue` would already short-circuit correctly with zero Jira round-trips — making the flag unnecessary. The plan's novelty is not justified against this simpler reuse of existing code.
findings.location: What > Section 2 (Outbound assignee by accountId); AC bullet 2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the required Jira permission for `/rest/api/3/user/search` ('Browse users and groups' global permission), document whether the existing service account has it, and add a structured log/alert when the endpoint returns 403 (not just silent degradation) so operators know the bootstrap is inoperative.
findings.finding: The plan's best-effort `/rest/api/3/user/search?query=<email>` endpoint (Section 3) is a new, previously-unused Jira Cloud REST surface in this codebase. Unlike `/user/assignable/search`, it requires the 'Browse users and groups' **global** permission. On Jira Cloud tenants where the service account lacks this global permission (a common restriction in managed/enterprise tenants), every bootstrap call will return HTTP 403, silently degrading to the existing search path — but the plan presents no acknowledgment of this permission requirement or whether it is already granted.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in What §1 what 'ticket id equals `local_assignee`' means in practice: specify whether this matches the full UUID, a short alias, or whether this branch exists as a fallback for teams that set assignee to an identity ticket's id/alias — and give a concrete example.
findings.finding: What §1 defines `jira_account_id` as 'find an identity whose **ticket id** equals `local_assignee`' — but `ticket_id` in this codebase means a UUID-shaped string (e.g. `264f-673a-e821-4a82`), whereas `local_assignee` in practice is a display name, email alias, or short string. The matching rule is undefined in practical terms: it cannot be implemented correctly without knowing whether rebar ticket ids ever appear as the assignee field of local tickets.
findings.location: What §1 — '`jira_account_id(local_assignee: str, …)` — find an identity whose ticket id equals `local_assignee` OR whose `email` equals it'

findings.finding: AC4 states the reconciler should record a 'degradation note' when reporter cannot be set, but 'recorded' is not defined as a specific observable artifact — it could mean a Python log.warning, a structured field on the sync result, a written entry in a reconcile log file, or something else. This makes the AC partially effort-framed and not fully measurable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 ('skip it with a recorded degradation note and does not fail')
findings.suggested_fix: State: 'Degradation is recorded as a `log.warning("reporter not set: permission denied", jira_key=…)` and the reconcile result dict carries `reporter_skipped=True`; the test asserts both.'

findings.finding: The term 'ticket id' in What §1 (`jira_account_id` function: 'find an identity whose ticket id equals `local_assignee`') is undefined in this plan and not explained in the referenced 'ddbe' dependency summary. It is ambiguous whether 'ticket id' means the identity's own rebar entity ID, a username field, or some other attribute — implementers cannot derive the lookup key from the plan text alone.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'ticket id' with the exact field name from the ddbe identity entity schema (e.g., 'the identity entity's primary key / `id` field') and add a parenthetical to clarify: 'find an identity whose `<field>` equals `local_assignee` (the rebar-local identifier for this user)'

findings.finding: Section 2 leaves the accountId-shape detection protocol undefined: it offers two mutually exclusive mechanisms ('detect the accountId shape, or pass an explicit assignee_is_account_id/pre-resolved flag') without specifying which to use, and never defines what the accountId shape is (format, regex, length, prefix). The `acli.py::update_issue` implementation must make a concrete decision to skip re-resolution, but has no spec to implement against.
findings.location: What §2 — outbound assignee by accountId; `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli.py` update_issue (~line 354)
findings.suggested_fix: Recommend the explicit-flag approach (`assignee_already_resolved: bool = False` kwarg on `update_issue`) because it is unambiguous and immune to Jira accountId format changes. Add `acli.py::update_issue` and `batch_dispatch::update_one` to file_impact as needing the new kwarg, and update the AC2 test to assert that `validate_assignee_exists` is NOT called when the flag is set.

findings.finding: Section 2 explicitly leaves the implementation choice open: 'detect the accountId shape, or pass an explicit `assignee_is_account_id`/pre-resolved flag from the differ' — two structurally different options (fragile heuristic vs. explicit plumbing) with no decision. An executor must stop and choose, which is a real design fork with different maintenance implications.
findings.location: What §2 — Outbound assignee by accountId, last paragraph ('detect the accountId shape, or pass an explicit …')
findings.suggested_fix: Choose the explicit flag: 'Thread `assignee_already_resolved: bool` from `_assignee_resolver` through to `update_issue`; when `True`, skip the `validate_assignee_exists` call and pass the accountId directly.' This avoids coupling to Jira's accountId string format.

findings.finding: The `update_issue` double-resolution fix (§2, `acli.py`) leaves the detection approach ambiguous — 'detect the accountId shape, OR pass an explicit `assignee_is_account_id`/pre-resolved flag' — without specifying which to implement. Shape-detection is fragile (a local username matching the accountId regex would bypass resolution), while the flag approach is safe but requires threading state across the call boundary. The plan doesn't resolve this choice, creating an under-specified behavior change.
findings.suggested_fix: Choose the explicit flag approach: 'The differ passes `assignee_already_resolved: bool` to `update_issue`; when True, `update_issue` sets the accountId directly and skips `validate_assignee_exists`. Remove the shape-detection alternative entirely.' Add a corresponding AC: 'When `assignee_already_resolved=True`, `update_issue` does not call `validate_assignee_exists` (assert mock not called).'

findings.finding: The `/rest/api/3/user/search?query=<email>` bootstrap (Section 3) requires the 'Browse Users and Groups' global Jira permission, which is distinct from the project-scoped permissions already held for `/user/assignable/search`. The plan's only documented degradation paths are 'hidden email (privacy)' and 'no result' — a 403 (permission denied) from a service account that lacks Browse Users is not addressed. In a locked-down Jira org this will fire on every reconcile pass, not just for privacy-hidden emails.
findings.suggested_fix: In `acli_rest.py`, wrap the `/user/search` call in a try/except that catches `urllib.error.HTTPError` with codes 403 AND 404 (and empty-list response) as degradation signals. Document in Section 3 that the service account needs 'Browse Users and Groups' or the bootstrap is silently skipped.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
